stem: Why can a moving spin retain phase after a balanced gradient pair that rephases stationary spins?

options.text: It occupies different positions during the two gradient lobes
It has a different hydrogen gyromagnetic ratio
It cannot experience magnetic gradients
It completes T1 recovery during the first lobe

explanation: Stationary spins experience equal and opposite gradient areas at one location. Moving spins sample different field strengths over time, so the phase effects do not perfectly cancel.

stem: What is the goal of first-order gradient moment nulling?

options.text: To suppress every stationary spin
To rephase spins moving at constant velocity
To calculate proton density without an echo
To correct motion with arbitrary acceleration and turbulence

explanation: Additional gradient lobes are designed so both stationary and constant-velocity spins have zero net phase at the echo. Higher-order or complex motion is not fully corrected.

stem: What image effect can flow compensation reduce?

options.text: T1 saturation caused by short TR
Chemical shift caused by fat-water resonance differences
Phase dispersion and ghosting from smoothly moving blood or CSF
Aliasing caused by an undersized field of view

explanation: Gradient moment nulling reduces coherent motion-related phase shifts and can restore signal in vessels or CSF. It does not correct unrelated sampling or chemical-shift artifacts.

stem: What is a common tradeoff of adding gradient moment nulling?

options.text: Elimination of all gradient switching
Mandatory reduction of field strength
Complete loss of stationary tissue signal
Longer minimum TE

explanation: The extra gradient lobes require time and can lengthen the minimum echo time. That may increase T2-star or motion-related signal loss in other parts of the sequence.

stem: Which flow pattern is least likely to be fully corrected by first-order gradient moment nulling?

options.text: Accelerating or turbulent flow
Stationary tissue
Constant-velocity laminar flow
A static phantom at isocenter

explanation: First-order compensation assumes constant velocity. Acceleration, pulsatility, and turbulence introduce higher-order and intravoxel phase behavior that remains uncorrected.
